items.front: 6-6-1: Basic vertical separation rule — when may an altitude be reassigned?
items.back: After the aircraft previously at that altitude has REPORTED LEAVING the altitude.

items.front: 6-6-1: Phraseology — what report to request when aircraft position vs lowest usable flight level is unknown?
6-6-2: Four exceptions requiring the stricter altitude reassignment rule
items.back: 1. Severe turbulence. 2. Military aerial refueling. 3. Pilot's-discretion climb/descent. 4. Cruise clearance. Must wait until aircraft reports at/passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minimum.

items.front: 6-6-2c2: Why can't Mode C be used for separation with a cruise-cleared aircraft?
items.back: Cruise clearance assigns a block from MIA to cruising altitude with pilot-discretion movement. Mode C cannot confirm verbal commitment to leave an altitude. Use pilot verbal reports only.

items.front: 6-6-2 note: Cruise clearance — can pilot return to a verbally-left altitude?
items.back: No. Once the pilot verbally reports leaving an altitude in descent, he/she MAY NOT return to that altitude.
Pilots must be in DIRECT RADIO COMMUNICATION with each other during climb/descent and CONCUR. Authorize the LOWER aircraft if CLIMBING, or the UPPER aircraft if DESCENDING.
